does: This paper analyzes the vulnerability of machine unlearning verification to adversarial data forging attacks. It formally characterizes ε-forging sets—collections of data points whose gradients approximate a target gradient within tolerance ε—and bounds their Lebesgue measure. The authors prove that for linear regression and shallow neural networks, the measure of forging sets scales as ε and can reach ε^d for sufficiently small ε. Under general regularity assumptions, they establish that forging set measure decays as ε^((d-r)/2), where d is data dimensionality and r represents the dimension of singular vectors corresponding to small singular values in a variation matrix. These results quantify how difficult it is to forge unlearning through gradient manipulation.
connects: This work addresses a fundamental verification challenge in machine unlearning, building on recent research into privacy-preserving model modifications and certified unlearning. It extends prior analysis of gradient-based unlearning verification by formalizing the adversarial forging problem—how bad actors might create fake data to simulate successful unlearning. The paper connects to broader work on model robustness, adversarial examples, and privacy attacks, applying tools from differential geometry and measure theory to quantify the hardness of crafting deceptive data. It responds to growing recognition that unlearning claims require robust verification mechanisms, building on foundational work in machine unlearning that largely assumed trustworthy verification.
enables: These theoretical bounds provide rigorous foundations for designing verifiable unlearning mechanisms robust to forging attacks. The measure-theoretic framework enables development of verification protocols that exploit the sparsity of forging sets in high dimensions, particularly when ε is restricted to small values. The results suggest practical strategies for tightening verification tolerance parameters and understanding when gradient-based unlearning claims become trustworthy. Future work can build on this foundation to develop computational methods for detecting or preventing forging, extend the analysis to deeper networks and more complex model architectures, and translate these theoretical insights into verifiable unlearning guarantees that meet privacy regulation requirements while remaining resistant to deceptive data manipulation.
